items.prompt: What does the CAP theorem claim about consistency, availability, and partition tolerance?
choices: A distributed system cannot guarantee all three simultaneously
Every system must always provide all three forever
Only single-node databases can have partitions
Availability is irrelevant when networks fail
items.explain: Section 12.2.7 states it is impossible to achieve all three guarantees simultaneously.

items.prompt: Under a network partition, AP systems may accept traffic and reconcile replicas later.
items.explain: The chapter contrasts CP (may reject writes) with AP (accept and reconcile later).

items.prompt: According to Example 12.10, why shard customer tables by geography or customer ID?
choices: So each node owns a slice of keys and write load spreads across the cluster
So every node stores an identical full copy of all keys only
So sharding becomes unnecessary for all workloads
So CAP no longer applies to any store
items.explain: Example 12.10 describes sharding so each node owns a key slice and load spreads.

items.prompt: Why prefer strong consistency in financial ledgers (Example 12.11)?
items.rubric: Mention avoiding conflicting account states / concurrent transfers / integrity.
items.sample_answer: So concurrent transfers cannot leave accounts in conflicting states for different clients.

items.prompt: Example 12.13 states DynamoDB and Cassandra commonly emphasize availability with eventual consistency tunable by quorums.
items.explain: Example 12.13 lists those stores as availability-first with tunable eventual consistency.
